title: Thermodynamic Geometry of two-dimensional square-well fluids
abstract: Thermodynamic geometry of two-dimensional fluids has been investigated using a square-well model as a prototype fluid. A comparison with the three-dimensional case is performed in the subcritical and supercritical domains of thermodynamic space. In the subcritical region, it is found that the R-crossing method has a narrower range of validity for two-dimensional fluids compared to three-dimensional ones. On the other hand, in the supercritical region, an analysis of different Widom lines, including the R Widom line, shows that for two-dimensional fluids these lines extend further into the supercritical region than their three-dimensional counterparts. A similar behavior is observed for the validity of the Clausius--Clapeyron equation in two-dimensional fluids.
summary: This paper compares the thermodynamic geometry of 2D and 3D square-well fluids. It finds that while 2D systems show less accurate phase coexistence identification via R-crossing, their supercritical crossover lines (Widom lines) extend deeper into the supercritical regime than in 3D. The study highlights how reduced dimensionality enhances fluctuations and alters thermodynamic landscapes.
main_problem: The study investigates how reduced dimensionality (2D vs 3D) affects the thermodynamic geometry and the behavior of phase transition indicators in square-well fluids.
main_result: In 2D fluids, the R-crossing method is less accurate than in 3D, but Widom lines and the validity of the Clausius-Clapeyron equation extend further into the supercritical region due to enhanced fluctuations.
method: The authors use Ruppeiner's thermodynamic geometry formalism, analyzing the scalar curvature and response functions derived from Helmholtz free energy equations of state.
model_or_system: The study uses two-dimensional and three-dimensional square-well fluids as prototype models for studying phase transitions and thermodynamic landscapes.
key_observables: Ruppeiner scalar curvature (R), isobaric heat capacity (cp), thermal expansion coefficient (ap), and speed of sound (cs).
important_parameters: Potential range (lambda), reduced temperature (Tr), reduced density (rho), and reduced pressure (Pr).
main_assumptions: The analysis is limited to the square-well potential, which is discontinuous, and the validity of the 3D and 2D equations of state is restricted to specific ranges of the potential range lambda.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows critical values as a function of potential range; Figure 2 displays scalar curvature isotherms; Figure 3 compares phase coexistence lines via R-crossing vs ground truth; Figures 4-7 show extrema of response functions and curvature in 2D and 3D; Figure 8 shows pressure fits near the critical point.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the thermodynamic geometry framework, defines the 2D and 3D square-well models, presents critical behavior results, compares subcritical R-crossing validity, analyzes supercritical Widom lines, and concludes with implications for dimensionality and future continuous potential models.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Behavior of critical values as a function of the potential range for 2D and 3D square-well systems. From top to bottom: critical temperature, critical density, and critical pressure.

Figure 2 presents the isotherms of the scalar curvature in the subcritical region for all the ranges considered in this study for 2D and 3D fluids. Divergences in the scalar curvature, which indicate the boundaries of the stability region for the fluids, are clearly observed. From these data, the complete spinodal curve can be extracted, since the positions of the divergences correspond exactly to points on this curve. Notably, the primary differences among these isotherms become evident at shorter ranges. For the larger equivalent ranges, the curves are nearly indistinguishable. This behavior can be understood be- cause, for these ranges, the system is near the mean-field limit, where a...

Fig. 2: Isotherms of the scalar curvature in the subcritical region at reduced temperature Tr = 0.90. In all plots, solid lines correspond to 2D square-well systems, and dashed red lines correspond to 3D square-well systems for the following ranges: a) λ2D, 3D = 1.2; b) λ2D, 3D = 1.5; c) λ2D, 3D = 2.0; d) λ2D,3D = 3.0.

Fig. 3: Vapor–liquid coexistence line for 2D and 3D square-well systems. Solid black lines were calculated using the thermody- namic procedure, while dashed red lines were obtained using the R-crossing method.

Fig. 4: Vapor–liquid coexistence line and extreme values of the response functions. Solid black lines were calculated using the thermodynamic procedure, while dashed red lines were ob- tained using the R-crossing method. For the extreme values: blue line, maximum of αp; brown line, maximum of cp; green line, minimum of R; and violet line, minimum of the sound velocity.
